critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experiment. The temporal split is correct, preprocessing discipline is explicitly stated, the baseline is reasonably matched, and the scope is honest about what is and is not being established. I'll analyze the genuine methodological concerns I can identify.

**Issue 1: Rolling feature computation at prediction time implies a 7-day look-ahead that may not exist in real deployment — but more critically, it creates a subtle leakage risk at the boundary of the training/validation split.**

The claim is that rolling statistics (7-day and 14-day rolling averages of airport delay rates, route delay rates) are computed using 'only historical data up to and including the current flight's scheduled time.' This is stated but its correctness depends entirely on implementation. The specific failure mode: if rolling windows are computed over the full dataset before the train/val split, then a flight scheduled on day 1 of the validation set (month 25, day 1) has its 14-day rolling average computed using data from month 24, days 17–31 — which is training data. That part is fine. But if the rolling computation is done naively on a single dataframe sorted by time, this is correct. The real concern is whether the rolling window implementation actually respects the temporal boundary at the flight level, not just the split level. This is more of an implementation concern than a design flaw, but it is a plausible silent misconfiguration.

More substantively: the 7-day rolling delay average at an airport is itself a feature that encodes 'what was happening at this airport in the last week.' This is a highly autocorrelated signal. A model that simply learns 'airports that were delayed last week tend to be delayed this week' may be doing very little multivariate integration and the AUC gain over the baseline may not reflect weather/demand interaction learning. The ablation (feature group 4 removed) partially addresses this, but the demand/congestion features (group 2) include these rolling averages and the experiment does not ablate them independently from the more theoretically interesting multivariate interactions.

Evidence: Run the ablation with demand features specifically being the rolling delay statistics only (no runway occupancy, no flight counts), and compare to the full demand feature set. If rolling-delay-alone recovers most of the gain, the model is primarily doing autocorrelation exploitation, not multivariate learning.

**Issue 2: The baseline is structurally weaker than stated, because airport-day-of-week-hour cell means with holiday offsets cannot represent interaction effects that are present in the simplest non-parametric models.**

The claim is that 'any gain by the boosted model must reflect learning of demand/weather interactions rather than differences in available data.' This is too strong. The baseline cannot represent interactions between features even in principle — it is a marginal mean estimator. A logistic regression with interaction terms using the same features as the boosted model would be a better baseline for isolating whether gradient boosting's structural advantages (non-linearity, high-order interactions) matter, versus simply having access to weather and demand jointly. The current design conflates two things: (a) having access to more features, and (b) being able to model non-linear interactions between those features. Even though the baseline gets equivalent 'tuning effort,' it is tuned over aggregation granularities, not over modeling capacity. A linear model with the same feature set would disentangle these.

Evidence: Add a logistic regression baseline with the full feature set. If the boosted model beats logistic regression substantially, the non-linearity claim is supported. If it barely beats it, the gain comes from feature access, not model architecture.

**Issue 3: The 15-minute threshold for the binary label is fixed, but the AUC is threshold-independent — this creates a tension where good calibration at the chosen threshold does not guarantee good calibration across the threshold range AUC integrates over.**

This is not a fatal flaw, but the choice of ECE as a calibration metric applied to a binary outcome defined at a single threshold means calibration is measured at the operating point, while AUC averages performance across all thresholds. If the model is miscalibrated in the tails (very high or very low predicted probability regions) but well-calibrated near 0.2 (the approximate base rate), AUC can be high and ECE can be low simultaneously, yet operational decisions at unusual thresholds (e.g., triggering expensive interventions only above 80% predicted probability) would be systematically wrong. This is a metric gap, not a design failure.

Evidence: Report reliability diagrams (calibration curves) rather than scalar ECE, particularly examining the high-confidence bins (>0.7 predicted probability) which correspond to the operationally actionable cases.

**Issue 4: Secondary time-blocked validation (months 13–18 held out) is described as a robustness check, but it is not clear whether the model was trained with or without those months.**

The claim is that this confirms 'stable performance across different time periods.' But if the model for this check is trained on months 1–12 and 19–24 (with months 13–18 withheld), the training data has a structural gap that does not represent normal model training. If the model is trained on months 1–24 inclusive (the standard training set), then months 13–18 are not held out — they are already in the training set. The design description does not clarify which case applies. If the former, then this is a genuinely independent temporal robustness check. If the latter, it is not a meaningful hold-out.

Evidence: Clarify the training set composition for this check. If months 13–18 are in the training set, this check provides no additional evidence and should be replaced with a true out-of-sample window.

**Summary:**
Issues 1 and 4 are implementation/design ambiguities that could silently inflate results. Issue 2 is a genuine confound in the experimental conclusion: the design cannot distinguish feature-access gains from architectural gains. Issue 3 is a metric gap relevant to the stated deployment use case. None of these individually destroy the experiment, but Issue 2 is a substantive methodological flaw in the causal claim being made.
all_issues_raised: Rolling lag features dominated by autocorrelation may explain most of the AUC gain without requiring multivariate interaction learning, and the ablation design does not isolate this.

The baseline is structurally incapable of representing interactions, so the experiment conflates feature-access gains with model-architecture gains; a same-feature logistic regression baseline is missing.
AUC averages over all thresholds while ECE is measured at a single operating point, leaving tail calibration (operationally important high-confidence predictions) unverified.

The secondary time-blocked validation (months 13–18) is ambiguous: if those months are included in standard training, the check is not a genuine hold-out and provides no additional robustness evidence.
